items.q: According to the inverted-U hypothesis, when is performance best?
items.correct: At a moderate, optimal level of arousal.
items.misconception: When arousal is as high as possible.
items.random_wrong: When arousal is at its lowest.
items.why: Too much arousal harms performance just as too little does; the peak sits in the middle of the curve.

items.q: Are cognitive and somatic anxiety the same thing?
items.correct: No - cognitive anxiety is mental (worry) while somatic anxiety is physical (e.g. raised heart rate).
items.misconception: Yes, anxiety is a single feeling.
items.random_wrong: Somatic is mental and cognitive is physical.
items.why: They are two distinct components, and mixing them up reverses how each affects performance.

items.q: In Weiner's model, is effort stable or unstable?
items.correct: Unstable - effort can change from one performance to the next.
items.misconception: Stable, because it comes from within.
items.why: Effort is internal but changeable, so it is classed as unstable, unlike fixed ability.

items.q: According to the inverted-U hypothesis, performance is best when arousal is:
items.why: The answer examiners want is: At an optimal, moderate level. “As low as possible” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Which is a symptom of somatic anxiety?
items.why: Correct answer: Raised heart rate and sweating. “Worrying thoughts” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: In Weiner's attribution model, 'ability' is classified as:
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Internal and stable. “External and unstable” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Cognitive anxiety typically affects performance by:
items.correct: Having a negative effect as worry increases
items.why: Correct answer: Having a negative effect as worry increases. “Improving it as worry grows” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.
